paragraph: Researchers at the Halvorsen Institute for Genomic Medicine in Trondheim announced on March 14th the successful application of their proprietary base-editing platform, designated BEX-7, to correct a single-nucleotide variant in the GLP-7Delta gene associated with congenital hypercholesterolemia. In a cohort of 88 patients enrolled at the Halvorsen Medical Center, the BEX-7 system achieved 91.3 percent on-target correction efficiency with an off-target indel rate of just 0.4 percent across 2.1 million assessed genomic loci. Serum low-density lipoprotein levels declined by an average of 64.7 percent at the 24-week follow-up assessment, with 71 of 88 patients reaching target cholesterol thresholds without adjunct pharmacotherapy. The delivery mechanism employed lipid nanoparticles with a novel ionizable head group, designated IHG-19, that achieved hepatic tropism exceeding 97 percent in accompanying murine models. Lead investigator Dr. Annika Fjeldstad stated in the accompanying preprint submitted to the Trondheim Journal of Translational Genomics that the BEX-7 platform represents the first base-editor capable of operating at temperatures below 35 degrees Celsius without loss of deaminase activity, a property attributed to a thermostabilizing mutation at position 214 of the engineered adenine deaminase domain.

A phase I clinical trial conducted at the Veltman Center for Rare Diseases in Ghent has yielded promising early results for a recombinant adeno-associated viral vector designated rAAV-VL9, engineered to deliver a functional copy of the MRFT-3 gene to skeletal muscle tissue in patients suffering from Veltman myopathic dystrophy, a rare autosomal recessive condition affecting approximately 1 in 180,000 live births across the European population. Twenty-four patients between the ages of 8 and 31 received a single intravenous infusion at doses ranging from 1.2 times 10 to the 13th to 4.8 times 10 to the 14th vector genomes per kilogram of body weight. At the 12-month assessment, patients at the highest dose cohort demonstrated a median 58.2 percent improvement on the Veltman Functional Muscle Scale, with transaminase elevations observed in 4 of 24 participants that resolved within six weeks following corticosteroid administration. Muscle biopsy samples obtained at week 16 revealed MRFT-3 protein expression at 44 percent of levels observed in age-matched unaffected controls, as quantified by immunofluorescence densitometry. The findings were presented by principal investigator Dr. Pieter Vanderhoeven at the 39th Symposium of the European Consortium for Inherited Muscle Disorders, held in Ghent on October 7th.

Scientists at the Quimera Biotechnology campus in Santiago have unveiled a next-generation CRISPR interference platform, termed CRISPRi-Q4, capable of suppressing the transcriptional output of up to 12 target genes simultaneously within a single delivery event. The system employs a catalytically dead version of the Cas12b ortholog isolated from the thermophilic bacterium Thermobacillus quimerensis, paired with a multiplex guide RNA scaffold encoding 12 independent spacer sequences separated by tRNA-derived linker elements averaging 76 nucleotides each. In experiments conducted on HepG2-derived organoids modeling nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, simultaneous suppression of the LPX-9, LPX-11, and FMR-Delta gene cluster reduced lipid droplet accumulation by 73.8 percent relative to scrambled-guide controls, as measured by BODIPY 493/503 fluorescence quantification. Off-target transcriptome-wide effects were assessed via bulk RNA sequencing at 50 million reads per sample across 18 organoid replicates; fewer than 14 non-target transcripts exhibited greater than 1.5-fold change in expression, representing a false-discovery-rate-corrected q-value below 0.03. Dr. Catalina Rojas-Mendes, the project's senior architect, indicated that the CRISPRi-Q4 cassette, at 5,840 base pairs, fits within the packaging capacity of standard rAAV serotypes without truncation of terminal repeat sequences.

The Brennfeld Institute of Epigenomics in Cologne has published findings demonstrating that targeted DNA methylation editing via a dCas9-DNMT3A fusion protein, designated MethEdit-C2, can stably silence the oncogenic long non-coding RNA HOTRL-22 in colorectal cancer organoids derived from 31 patient biopsies. Methylation of a 340-base-pair CpG island upstream of the HOTRL-22 transcriptional start site reduced steady-state transcript abundance by 88.6 percent as measured by droplet digital PCR seven days post-transfection. Organoid proliferation indices, assessed by EdU incorporation assay, fell by 61.2 percent relative to unedited controls, and apoptosis rates as measured by caspase-3/7 luminescence increased 4.3-fold. Importantly, a companion methylation array covering 865,000 CpG sites genome-wide revealed no statistically significant off-target hypermethylation events at a Bonferroni-corrected alpha of 0.05. The MethEdit-C2 construct was delivered via an electroporation protocol optimized for organoid cultures using a square-wave pulse of 1,400 volts applied for 30 milliseconds. Senior author Dr. Lena Bergstroh stated that the epigenetic silencing was maintained through at least 28 passages spanning 112 days of continuous culture, suggesting heritable propagation through cell division without reapplication of the editing agent.

A consortium of eight research institutions coordinated by the Paracelsus Genome Alliance in Vienna has released the first comprehensive long-read sequencing atlas of structural variants in the Caucasian-descent population, cataloguing 4.2 million insertions, deletions, and inversions with a median size of 1,840 base pairs across 12,500 whole-genome sequences generated on the Nanopore PacMax-II platform. The study identified 317 novel structural variants overlapping known disease-associated regulatory elements, including a 5.3-kilobase inversion in the promoter region of the CFTR-homolog CLRN-9 found at a population frequency of 0.0034, which co-segregated with a clinical bronchiectasis phenotype in 23 of 31 carrier families assessed. Read lengths averaged 28,400 base pairs with N50 values of 41,200 base pairs, enabling phased assembly of 96.7 percent of the assessed genomes into haplotype-resolved contigs. The dataset, to be deposited in the Paracelsus Variant Repository under accession series PVR-2024-0041 through PVR-2024-12500, will serve as a reference panel for imputation of rare structural variants in future genome-wide association studies. Coordinating investigator Dr. Markus Albrecht-Weyler estimated the total sequencing effort required 14.3 petabases of raw data generated over 22 months at eight distributed sequencing centers.

Investigators at the Mira Lund Center for mRNA Therapeutics in Copenhagen have reported that a chemically modified mRNA encoding the secreted enzyme ALPH-4 reduced accumulated substrate by 82.1 percent in a murine model of Lund storage disorder when administered intravenously at a dose of 0.5 milligrams per kilogram once weekly for eight weeks. The mRNA molecule incorporated N1-methylpseudouridine at all uridine positions and a proprietary 5-prime cap analog designated CapX-7, which increased translational efficiency 3.8-fold relative to standard anti-reverse cap analogs in CHO cell transient transfection assays. In vivo half-life of the encoded enzyme, as measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay in serum samples taken at 2, 6, 12, and 24 hours post-injection, averaged 18.4 hours across the eight-week treatment period with no detectable decline attributable to immune-mediated clearance. Liver biopsies collected at week 9 demonstrated lysosomal clearance of accumulated ALPH-4 substrate in 94 percent of hepatocytes examined by transmission electron microscopy. Dr. Sofie Dalgaard-Moller, the study's lead pharmacologist, reported that toxicological screening including complete blood count, serum chemistry, and histopathological evaluation of major organs revealed no adverse findings at therapeutic doses, supporting advancement to an investigational new drug application.

Researchers at the Takimoto Institute for Functional Genomics in Osaka have described a novel prime editing strategy, designated PE-T5, for correcting the 11-nucleotide deletion in exon 7 of the KCNR-18 potassium channel gene responsible for Takimoto episodic ataxia, an autosomal dominant channelopathy affecting an estimated 4,200 patients nationwide. The PE-T5 system employs an engineered reverse transcriptase domain fused to a Cas9 nickase variant containing five point mutations that collectively increase fidelity 22-fold relative to the canonical PE2 architecture. In iPSC-derived cerebellar Purkinje neurons carrying the heterozygous KCNR-18 deletion, PE-T5 achieved 38.7 percent allele conversion at the target locus, as confirmed by amplicon deep sequencing at 8,000-fold coverage, with unintended indel formation at fewer than 0.3 percent of edited alleles. Electrophysiological recordings from corrected neurons showed restoration of delayed rectifier current density to 76 percent of wild-type levels, compared with 31 percent in unedited disease neurons. The complete correction cassette, at 7,120 base pairs excluding homology arms, was packaged into a split-intein dual-AAV system and demonstrated co-transduction efficiency of 84 percent in mouse cerebellar slice preparations following stereotaxic injection.

A team led by Dr. Rashida Oyelaran at the Abuja Institute for Sickle Cell Research has demonstrated that a single infusion of autologous hematopoietic stem cells edited ex vivo with the CRISPR-Cas9 nuclease system to reactivate fetal hemoglobin via disruption of the BCL11A erythroid enhancer analog BLCH-3A resulted in sustained therapeutic hemoglobin F levels in 19 of 22 patients with sickle cell disease followed for a median of 18 months. Edited cells were manufactured using a closed-system electroporation device and reinfused following busulfan-based myeloablative conditioning at doses of 3.2 milligrams per kilogram per day for four consecutive days. Hemoglobin F fraction increased from a pre-treatment median of 4.1 percent to a post-treatment median of 38.6 percent at month 12, reducing mean annual sickle pain crisis rates from 7.4 to 0.3 events per patient-year. Bone marrow chimerism analysis at 12 months demonstrated that edited cells represented a median 74.8 percent of the hematopoietic stem cell compartment. Three patients experienced grade 3 cytopenias during engraftment that resolved by day 45 post-infusion. The findings were submitted to the West African Journal of Hematological Gene Medicine and are currently under peer review.

Scientists at the Aldenmoor Sequencing Consortium in Edinburgh have introduced a new short-read sequencing chemistry, designated SynthSeq-R4, achieving a raw base accuracy of 99.97 percent at a throughput of 3.2 terabases per instrument run on the updated FlowCell-Nano platform. The chemistry relies on a reversible terminator nucleotide series featuring a novel 3-prime azidomethyl protecting group that reduces photocleavage latency to 8 milliseconds, enabling a 22 percent increase in sequencing cycle speed relative to prior-generation terminators. In a benchmarking study spanning 48 whole-genome sequencing libraries prepared from Coriell reference samples, SynthSeq-R4 achieved a single-nucleotide polymorphism sensitivity of 99.91 percent and specificity of 99.98 percent against ground-truth genotypes. Coverage uniformity, measured as the fraction of bases covered at greater than 20-fold depth, reached 97.8 percent at a sequencing depth of 30-fold genome coverage. Dr. Callum Farthingale, director of chemistry development at the Consortium, indicated that SynthSeq-R4 is projected to reduce per-genome sequencing costs by 31 percent relative to the incumbent SynthSeq-R3 chemistry, owing to a 40 percent reduction in fluorescent dye consumption per cycle. Commercial availability is anticipated in the second quarter of next year through a licensing arrangement with three major instrument manufacturers.

The Varga Institute for Developmental Epigenetics in Budapest has published a comprehensive single-cell ATAC-sequencing analysis of chromatin accessibility dynamics across 14 discrete stages of human pancreatic organoid differentiation, generating profiles from 186,000 individual nuclei processed using a microfluidic droplet platform. The study identified 34,200 stage-specific open chromatin regions, of which 8,900 were found exclusively during the transition from pancreatic progenitor to endocrine precursor identity occurring between differentiation days 9 and 14. Motif enrichment analysis revealed significant overrepresentation of binding sequences for the transcription factors NKX4-7 and PDX-homolog PXDM-2 at loci associated with the INSA-1 and INSA-3 insulin-related gene cluster, with enrichment scores of 18.4 and 14.7 respectively. Integration of these accessibility data with matched H3K27ac ChIP-sequencing profiles across six differentiation stages identified 612 putative enhancer elements that gain activity specifically during beta-cell specification, 47 of which harbor common single-nucleotide polymorphisms associated with type 2 diabetes risk in prior genome-wide association datasets. Dr. Eszter Varga-Nemeth, the consortium's lead bioinformatician, reported that the complete dataset, comprising 4.1 terabytes of processed files, has been deposited in the Varga Epigenomic Database under study accession VED-2024-0188.

Investigators at the Schreiber Center for Translational Virology in Zurich have developed a novel AAV capsid variant, designated AAV-SCZ11, by directed evolution through six rounds of selection for tropism toward dopaminergic neurons of the substantia nigra in non-human primate models. The capsid was generated from a library of 2.4 million variants created via error-prone PCR and DNA shuffling of six parental AAV serotype capsid sequences. Following intravenous administration at a dose of 5 times 10 to the 13th vector genomes per kilogram in four cynomolgus macaques, AAV-SCZ11 transduced 86.1 percent of tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, as assessed by immunofluorescence confocal microscopy on post-mortem tissue sections. Liver transduction, a frequent off-target concern for systemic AAV delivery, was reduced 14-fold relative to the AAV9 parent serotype under matched dosing conditions. Immunogenicity profiling revealed that serum neutralizing antibody titers against AAV-SCZ11 were detectable in only 2 of 4 animals at 90 days post-injection, with titers remaining below 1:80 dilution in both cases. The investigators propose AAV-SCZ11 as a platform capsid for future gene therapy studies targeting Parkinson-spectrum neurodegeneration.

A research group at the Florentin University Department of Transcriptomics in Lyon has published spatial transcriptomic maps of gene expression in human cardiac tissue from 22 donors with dilated cardiomyopathy and 18 age-matched controls, using a probe-based in situ sequencing platform capable of simultaneously detecting 6,000 transcripts per tissue section. The analysis revealed a 7.2-fold upregulation of the stress-responsive long non-coding RNA CARDLNC-5 in cardiomyocytes within the fibrotic border zone relative to remote myocardium in diseased samples, a finding not previously reportable by bulk RNA sequencing due to cell-type compositional differences. Neighboring cell type interaction modeling using a ligand-receptor co-localization algorithm identified aberrant signaling between activated cardiac fibroblasts expressing TGFR-homolog TGRH-4 and adjacent cardiomyocytes in diseased tissue, with a predicted interaction score 3.9-fold above background. The spatial dataset, generated from 264 tissue sections with a median spot resolution of 55 micrometers, identified 18 spatially variable gene modules not previously reported in cardiac transcriptomic studies. Principal investigator Dr. Margaux Fontaine-Delacroix indicated that her team plans to integrate these spatial data with single-nucleus ATAC-sequencing profiles from matched donors to construct a multi-omic regulatory atlas of human dilated cardiomyopathy.

The Okonkwo Foundation for Genomic Equity in Lagos has announced completion of the largest whole-genome sequencing study of individuals with African ancestry conducted to date, encompassing 24,800 participants from 18 countries across Sub-Saharan Africa and sequenced to a mean depth of 35-fold coverage using the SynthSeq-R3 short-read platform. The resulting variant catalogue contains 312 million single-nucleotide polymorphisms and 21 million small insertions and deletions, of which 48 percent are not present in any existing public genomic reference database. Genome-wide association analysis for 14 common cardiometabolic traits identified 91 novel loci reaching genome-wide significance at a p-value threshold of 5 times 10 to the negative 8th power, including a cluster of variants in a 400-kilobase region near the APOV-3 gene associated with serum triglyceride levels exhibiting odds ratios ranging from 1.18 to 1.34. Population structure analysis revealed 23 genetically distinct sub-populations not previously captured in global reference panels, with FST values between adjacent clusters ranging from 0.007 to 0.041. Dr. Ngozi Okonkwo-Adeyemi, the foundation's scientific director, stated that all variant-level summary statistics will be deposited in an open-access repository concurrent with publication, with individual-level data accessible to qualified researchers under a data access committee framework.

Scientists at the Westerberg Molecular Therapeutics Laboratory in Gothenburg have reported the first in vivo application of an epigenome editing approach targeting the promoter of the FMRX-2 gene in a mouse model of fragile-X-spectrum disorder. The intervention employed a dCas9-TET1 fusion protein, designated DemethEdit-W3, delivered via a dual-AAV intein-split system injected bilaterally into the hippocampus of adult male mice carrying a hypermethylated CGG repeat expansion of 210 repeats in the FMRX-2 5-prime UTR. DemethEdit-W3 achieved 63.2 percent demethylation of the target CpG island within 21 days of injection as measured by targeted bisulfite amplicon sequencing at 5,000-fold coverage. FMRX-2 mRNA expression, quantified by in situ hybridization chain reaction, recovered to 41 percent of wild-type levels in hippocampal CA1 neurons at four weeks post-injection. Behavioral testing using the Barnes maze and novel object recognition assay demonstrated statistically significant improvements in spatial memory and recognition memory, with effect sizes of 0.74 and 0.81 Cohen's d respectively relative to untreated littermate controls. The study's findings were presented at the Inaugural Westerberg Symposium on Neurogenetic Therapeutics on November 3rd and submitted concurrently to the Scandinavian Journal of Molecular Neuroscience.

A team at the Delacorte Institute of Protein Engineering in Montreal has engineered a compact Cas9 variant, designated miniCas9-DE4, through rational domain truncation and stabilizing substitutions, reducing the protein from its wild-type size of 1,368 amino acids to 790 amino acids while retaining 94.6 percent of on-target cleavage activity as measured in a human cell fluorescent reporter assay. The key truncation removed 204 amino acids from the REC2 and REC3 recognition lobes, guided by cryo-electron microscopy structures of the ternary Cas9-sgRNA-DNA complex resolved at 2.9 angstrom resolution. MiniCas9-DE4 demonstrated a protospacer adjacent motif preference of 5-prime NGG-3-prime identical to the canonical Streptococcus-derived parent enzyme. Critically, the entire miniCas9-DE4 coding sequence, including a nuclear localization signal and the bpA polyadenylation signal, fits within 3,180 base pairs, enabling single-construct AAV packaging alongside a U6-driven guide RNA expression cassette with 780 base pairs of remaining capacity for a 400-base-pair therapeutic transgene. In 78 HEK293T transfection experiments across 12 target loci, miniCas9-DE4 achieved a mean editing efficiency of 47.3 percent compared with 50.1 percent for full-length Cas9, a non-significant difference by two-sided t-test at alpha of 0.05. Dr. Amelie Tremblay-Fortin led the engineering effort and indicated that miniCas9-DE4 sequences have been deposited with Addgene for academic distribution.

Researchers at the Heilman Center for Inherited Metabolic Disease in Cincinnati have announced positive top-line results from a phase II/III pivotal trial of an investigational gene therapy, designated GTX-HC7, for propionic acidemia, a rare inborn error of metabolism caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in the PCCA-homolog PRPCA-1 or PCCB-homolog PRPCB-2 genes. The trial enrolled 54 pediatric patients between the ages of 3 and 16 at 11 centers across the United States, Canada, and Germany, with participants receiving a single intravenous infusion of rAAV8 encoding a codon-optimized PRPCA-1 cDNA under the control of the liver-specific ApoE-HCR promoter element at a fixed dose of 6 times 10 to the 13th vector genomes per kilogram. The primary endpoint, reduction in mean annual hospitalization for metabolic decompensation events at 12 months post-infusion, was met with a 72.4 percent reduction relative to the pre-treatment 24-month baseline period. Secondary endpoints included a 48.3 percent reduction in plasma propionylcarnitine concentrations and a 31.7 percent improvement on the Pediatric Quality of Life Inventory metabolic disease subscale. No serious adverse events attributable to GTX-HC7 were observed. The trial's data safety monitoring board recommended continuation to 24-month follow-up without protocol modification.

An international collaboration anchored at the Leuven Centre for Single-Cell Genomics has published an atlas of 4.1 million single-cell transcriptomes from human fetal tissues spanning 11 organ systems and 22 developmental time points ranging from 5 to 22 weeks of gestation. The atlas, generated using a droplet-based library preparation protocol modified to handle post-mortem fetal tissue with RNA integrity numbers as low as 4.2, identified 412 provisional cell type clusters representing previously undescribed intermediate progenitor states across kidney, liver, gut, brain, and thymus lineages. Of particular significance, a population of 18,400 cells annotated as hepatoblast-erythroblast transitional progenitors expressed a unique co-regulatory module comprising 47 transcription factor genes including the novel factor HBXT-3, expression of which was undetectable in any post-natal human tissue in comparative adult single-cell datasets. Trajectory inference using the RNA velocity method estimated a differentiation velocity coefficient of 0.34 for this population, consistent with rapid fate commitment. Data from all 22 developmental time points, totaling 96 terabytes of raw FASTQ files and 4.8 terabytes of processed count matrices, have been deposited in the European Fetal Genomics Archive under study identifier EGA-FGA-2024-0007.

Scientists at the Rennfeld Biotechnology Institute in Hamburg have engineered a series of synthetic transcription activator-like effector nuclease proteins, collectively designated TALEN-RFB, targeting the aberrant splice acceptor site created by an intronic pseudoexon-activating variant in the DYSF-homolog DYSH-4 gene responsible for Rennfeld limb-girdle myopathy type 2B. The TALEN-RFB proteins were designed using a revised RVD code incorporating three non-canonical recognition modules predicted by machine learning models trained on 1.4 million TALEN-DNA binding affinity measurements. Transfection of TALEN-RFB into myoblasts from six affected donors achieved 53.7 percent disruption of the pseudoexon splice acceptor as measured by RT-PCR and capillary electrophoresis, restoring correctly spliced DYSH-4 transcript to 68.4 percent of levels observed in unaffected control myoblasts. Immunofluorescence staining of differentiated myotubes confirmed restoration of DYSH-4 protein to the sarcolemma in 61 percent of myonuclei examined. Transcriptome-wide RNA sequencing at 100 million reads per sample across 24 libraries from three donors revealed no significant exon-skipping or cryptic splicing events at off-target loci meeting a false-discovery-rate threshold of 0.05. The TALEN-RFB design pipeline has been made publicly available through a web application hosted by the Rennfeld Bioinformatics Group.

The Karimov Institute of Molecular Oncology in Tashkent has reported that a lipid nanoparticle formulation delivering circular RNA encoding the tumor suppressor PTENL-7, a naturally occurring long isoform of the PTEN-homolog expressed preferentially in glioblastoma-resistant astrocyte populations, reduced tumor volume by 78.3 percent in orthotopic xenograft models of glioblastoma multiforme compared with empty-vector controls over a 28-day treatment period. The circular RNA, produced via group I intron-mediated back-splicing and purified to greater than 95 percent circularity by RNase R treatment followed by HPLC fractionation, exhibited a half-life of 62 hours in U87-MG glioblastoma cells, compared with 6.4 hours for the analogous linear mRNA construct. Lipid nanoparticles were surface-functionalized with a transferrin receptor-binding peptide designated TRP-K4 to enhance blood-brain barrier transcytosis, achieving a brain-to-liver distribution ratio of 2.3 following tail vein injection in immunocompromised mice. Proteomic analysis of treated tumor tissue identified 284 differentially abundant proteins relative to vehicle-treated controls, with the top-ranked pathway being PI3K-AKT-homolog signaling, suppressed at a normalized enrichment score of negative 3.4. Dr. Dilnoza Yusupova-Karimova presented the findings at the Central Asian Symposium for Translational Cancer Biology on September 22nd.

A collaborative team from the Bengtsson Nordic Institute of Human Genetics in Uppsala and the Praxis Center for Applied Genomics in Rotterdam has identified a recurrent de novo structural variant, designated DNV-sv1847, consisting of a 47-kilobase tandem duplication of exons 3 through 9 of the neurodevelopmental gene NRXN-homolog NRXH-5, in 34 of 6,200 whole-genome-sequenced individuals with autism spectrum disorder compared with zero occurrences among 14,500 neurotypical controls. The variant was undetectable by standard microarray comparative genomic hybridization at probe densities below 1 probe per 2 kilobases but was consistently identified by long-read sequencing at 15-fold coverage using the PacMax-II platform. Functional characterization in neurons differentiated from three de novo carrier iPSC lines demonstrated a 2.1-fold increase in NRXH-5 transcript abundance and a concomitant 38.4 percent reduction in inhibitory synapse density as measured by immunofluorescent quantification of VGAT-homolog VGAH-1 puncta per unit dendritic length. Electrophysiological recordings from 72-hour co-cultures of carrier neurons and wild-type astrocytes revealed a 44 percent reduction in miniature inhibitory postsynaptic current frequency. The investigators propose DNV-sv1847 as a high-confidence autism risk variant and are currently recruiting a replication cohort of 3,000 individuals across four Nordic countries.

Researchers at the Mendelsohn Institute for Translational Genetics in Tel Aviv have demonstrated that a cell-permeable peptide nucleic acid conjugate, designated PNA-TG22, targeting the G-quadruplex structure formed by the repeat expansion in the HEXC-1 gene associated with Mendelsohn hexosaminidase-C deficiency, reduced repeat-associated non-AUG translation by 81.4 percent in iPSC-derived motor neurons from four affected donors without detectable suppression of canonical HEXC-1 open reading frame translation. PNA-TG22 was conjugated to a mitochondria-targeting sequence to achieve cytoplasmic and nuclear co-localization, with subcellular distribution confirmed by fluorescence confocal microscopy showing 68 percent nuclear and 19 percent cytoplasmic signal at 24 hours post-treatment. In repeat expansion mouse models carrying 420 GGCGGC repeats, twice-weekly intrathecal injection of PNA-TG22 at 2.5 micrograms per injection for 12 weeks reduced foci-forming RNA in lumbar spinal cord neurons by 73.6 percent as detected by fluorescence in situ hybridization and improved grip strength by 31.4 percent relative to vehicle-treated controls. No off-target effects were detected in transcriptome-wide ribosome profiling experiments covering 18,400 expressed open reading frames. The study was submitted to the Mediterranean Journal of Neurogenetic Medicine on March 2nd.

The Portsmith Center for Functional Proteomics in Bristol has developed a CRISPR activation platform, designated CRISPRa-PB9, using a dCas9-VP64-p65-Rta tripartite activation domain that achieves a mean 28.7-fold induction of target gene expression when delivered with an optimized sgRNA scaffold incorporating a 5-prime-extended tetraloop hairpin that increases nuclear Cas9 stability by 2.4-fold as measured by chromatin immunoprecipitation. In a screen of 1,420 candidate regulators of hepatic lipid metabolism conducted in primary human hepatocytes from 8 donors, CRISPRa-PB9-mediated upregulation of LPCR-11, a previously uncharacterized locus encoding a putative lipid phosphatase, reduced intracellular triglyceride accumulation by 54.7 percent compared with non-targeting controls as measured by a colorimetric enzymatic assay. The screen used a pooled sgRNA library of 21,300 elements delivered at a multiplicity of infection of 0.3 to ensure single-integration events in greater than 93 percent of transduced cells. Transcriptomic analysis of LPCR-11-activated hepatocytes identified 318 differentially expressed genes at a false-discovery rate below 0.05, with the strongest downstream effects on fatty acid beta-oxidation pathway genes including ACADVL-homolog ACVL-3 and HADH-homolog HDHA-7. The findings were communicated by Dr. Harriet Portsmith-Cawley at the European Liver Genomics Meeting in Bristol on February 18th.

Scientists at the Nakagawa Institute for Stem Cell Engineering in Kyoto have reported successful generation of vascularized kidney organoids using a co-differentiation protocol that introduces endothelial progenitor cells derived from a second iPSC line at differentiation day 12, resulting in organoids with a 3.1-fold greater nephron count and perfusable capillary networks averaging 34 micrometers in diameter and 2.4 millimeters in total vessel length per organoid, as measured by optical coherence tomography imaging. The protocol requires 24 days from iPSC to mature organoid and yields an average of 18 nephron equivalents per organoid, each containing proximal tubule, loop of Henle-equivalent, and glomerulus-like structures expressing the markers NPHS2-homolog NPHS2H, CUBN-homolog CUBN5, and UMOD-homolog UMDH-3 respectively. Drug-induced nephrotoxicity testing with cisplatin analogs at concentrations from 1 to 100 micromolar demonstrated dose-dependent loss of proximal tubule marker expression with an IC50 of 12.4 micromolar, closely matching values reported in primary human tubular epithelial cells. A panel of 14 nephrotoxic compounds tested at 10 micromolar concentration showed a sensitivity of 86 percent and specificity of 91 percent for clinical nephrotoxicity prediction. Dr. Keiko Nakagawa-Hirota indicated that the vascularized organoid system will be made commercially available through the institute's biotechnology licensing office by the end of the current fiscal year.

An investigation led by Dr. Valentina Ferri-Marchetti at the Torino Center for Mitochondrial Medicine has demonstrated that a targeted RNA base editing approach, designated RESTORE-T4, can correct a heteroplasmic mitochondrial DNA point mutation at position 9,185 of the mitochondrial genome responsible for Torino MELAS-spectrum syndrome in 28 of 35 patient-derived cybrid cell lines. The RESTORE-T4 system employs a mitochondrially targeted deaminase-containing protein designated MitoDeam-2 directed to the target position by a custom-designed mitochondria-localizing antisense RNA element of 41 nucleotides that hybridizes to the complementary strand of the mitochondrial genome. Heteroplasmy shifted from a mean of 84.3 percent mutant molecules at baseline to 29.7 percent mutant molecules at 14 days post-transfection as measured by droplet digital PCR with allele-discriminating hydrolysis probes. Mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I activity, measured spectrophotometrically in whole-cell lysates, increased by 57.3 percent in treated cybrids relative to untreated controls. Oxygen consumption rate as measured by microplate respirometry increased by 41.8 percent, and lactate-to-pyruvate ratios decreased by 38.2 percent, indicating improved oxidative phosphorylation capacity. The study was presented at the International Symposium on Mitochondrial Gene Editing in Torino on June 11th.

The Thornwall Biosciences Research Division in Auckland has completed a proof-of-concept study demonstrating that synthetic enhancer elements designed de novo using a generative deep learning model, designated GenEnhance-4, can drive tissue-specific transgene expression rivaling endogenous promoter activity in the targeted cell type. GenEnhance-4 was trained on a dataset of 2.8 million characterized enhancer sequences from the ENCODE-homolog ENCD-7 compendium annotated for activity in 44 human tissue types. A library of 3,600 computationally designed 500-base-pair synthetic enhancers targeting hepatocyte specificity was synthesized, cloned upstream of a reporter gene, and tested by massively parallel reporter assay in primary human hepatocytes and 11 non-hepatic cell lines. Of the 3,600 tested elements, 312 achieved hepatocyte-to-average-non-hepatic expression ratios exceeding 50:1, with the top performer, designated SynEnh-H7, achieving a ratio of 184:1 and absolute activity within 1.3-fold of the endogenous ApoE-HCR promoter element. Machine learning interpretation of the top-performing synthetic enhancers revealed convergent enrichment of binding motifs for the liver-enriched transcription factors HNF4-homolog HNF4H and CEBPA-homolog CEPAH-3. Dr. Aroha Ngata-Thornwall, the study's senior scientist, noted that SynEnh-H7 has been incorporated into the company's lead gene therapy vector program targeting ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency.

Investigators at the Grauberg Center for Transcription Factor Biology in Frankfurt have mapped the genome-wide binding landscape of the pioneering transcription factor FOXA-homolog FXAH-3 across eight stages of hepatic differentiation from human embryonic stem cells, generating chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing datasets at a mean depth of 42 million uniquely mapping reads per sample and identifying 124,000 FXAH-3 binding sites in aggregate across all stages. Analysis revealed that 22,400 binding sites were occupied transiently during the hepatic specification window between differentiation days 8 and 11, corresponding to a 3.8-fold enrichment at loci subsequently marked by H3K4me1 in mature hepatocytes relative to pluripotent cell H3K4me1 status, as assessed by integrated histone modification ChIP-sequencing. Motif co-enrichment analysis at transient FXAH-3 sites identified significant co-occurrence of binding motifs for GATA-homolog GATH-6 and ONECUT-homolog ONCUT-2, with p-values of 1.2 times 10 to the negative 18th and 3.4 times 10 to the negative 14th respectively. Perturbation of FXAH-3 expression by doxycycline-inducible degron tagging and depletion at each differentiation stage demonstrated that FXAH-3 is dispensable for hepatic progenitor formation but essential for albumin and ASGR-homolog ASGHR-1 expression in mature hepatocytes. The complete dataset has been deposited under accession GBG-2024-0712 in the Grauberg Functional Genomics Repository.

A team at the Esperanza Institute of Gene Regulation in Buenos Aires has identified a cluster of 14 microRNA species, collectively designated the miR-EBA cluster, encoded within a 12-kilobase intergenic region on chromosome equivalent 7q arm analog in the rat genome, whose combinatorial overexpression in neonatal rat cardiomyocytes reprogrammed towards a proliferative phenotype as evidenced by a 4.7-fold increase in EdU incorporation and 3.1-fold increase in Aurora B-homolog AURB-4 kinase puncta indicative of cytokinesis. In a rat model of myocardial infarction, intramyocardial injection of an AAV9 vector expressing the miR-EBA cluster at a dose of 1 times 10 to the 11th vector genomes per heart one week post-infarction resulted in a 19.4 percent improvement in left ventricular ejection fraction at 8 weeks post-infarction compared with 4.7 percent improvement in empty vector controls as measured by echocardiography. Histomorphometric analysis revealed a 34.2 percent reduction in scar area and a 28.6 percent increase in cardiomyocyte density in the infarct border zone of treated animals. Transcriptomic profiling of treated hearts identified upregulation of cell cycle entry genes including CCND-homolog CCNDH-2 and CDK4-homolog CDK4H-7, consistent with the proposed pro-proliferative mechanism. Dr. Gonzalo Espinoza-Varela led the study and indicated that translation to a large-animal porcine infarction model is planned for the upcoming fiscal year.

Scientists at the Pettifer-Holt Research Laboratories in Cambridge have unveiled a platform for programmable RNA splicing modulation using engineered CRISPR-associated RNA-binding proteins, designated CARP-PH3, derived from the Type VI CRISPR effector Cas13d fused to a series of splicing regulatory domains including exonic splicing enhancer and silencer peptide motifs. In a panel of 22 disease-relevant exon-skipping events tested in HEK293 cells, CARP-PH3 complexed with sequence-specific crRNA guides achieved a median exon inclusion shift of 44.8 percentage points, compared with 11.3 percentage points for the unmodified Cas13d protein lacking splicing domains. Importantly, the catalytic activity of CARP-PH3 was abolished by two point mutations in the HEPN domain, ensuring RNA binding without cleavage; transcriptome-wide CLASH analysis of RNA-protein interactions confirmed that guide-independent RNA engagement occurred at fewer than 0.08 percent of assessed splice junctions. The platform was applied to restore the skipping of the mutant exon 51 equivalent in a Duchenne muscular dystrophy analog gene, DYSTR-1, achieving 81.3 percent exon exclusion and restoration of a truncated reading frame in myoblasts from three affected donors. Protein immunoblot analysis confirmed restoration of a shortened but partially functional DYSTR-1 protein at 43 percent of wild-type molecular weight. Dr. Eleanor Pettifer-Holt presented the findings to the United Kingdom Splicing Biology Network annual meeting on April 5th.

The Yildiz Research Consortium in Istanbul has completed a comparative analysis of seven lipid nanoparticle formulations for hepatic mRNA delivery, evaluating delivery efficiency, innate immune activation, tolerability, and manufacturing scalability in parallel using a battery of standardized assays adapted from the Yildiz Nanoparticle Characterization Toolkit version 3.1. The seven formulations differed in ionizable lipid identity, phospholipid composition, cholesterol fraction, and PEG-lipid density. At a fixed mRNA dose of 0.3 milligrams per kilogram in BALB/c mice, hepatic luciferase expression 6 hours post-injection ranged from 2.1 times 10 to the 8th to 4.7 times 10 to the 9th photons per second across formulations, a 22-fold range. Serum cytokine profiling at 4 hours post-injection revealed that two formulations exhibiting the highest delivery efficiency also induced the greatest IL-6-homolog ILH-6 elevations, reaching peak concentrations of 380 and 440 picograms per milliliter respectively. A novel formulation designated LNP-YRC7 achieved the optimal balance, with hepatic expression of 3.8 times 10 to the 9th photons per second and peak ILH-6 of only 68 picograms per milliliter. Cryo-transmission electron microscopy of LNP-YRC7 revealed a multilamellar internal structure with electron-dense mRNA-containing compartments averaging 4.2 nanometers in thickness. Lead formulation chemist Dr. Selin Yildiz-Dogan reported that LNP-YRC7 demonstrated acceptable stability at 2 to 8 degrees Celsius storage for 6 months with less than 8 percent loss of mRNA encapsulation efficiency.

An international team coordinated by the Faustino Institute for Population Genomics in Lisbon has conducted a genome-wide polygenic score analysis for 12 complex traits in a trans-ethnic meta-analysis spanning 214,000 individuals from European, East Asian, South Asian, and West African ancestry groups, identifying 8 traits for which polygenic scores trained in European samples showed significantly attenuated predictive performance in non-European populations, with R-squared values declining by a mean of 51.3 percent. To address this disparity, the team developed a novel Bayesian polygenic scoring framework, designated PGS-FAI, that incorporates population-specific linkage disequilibrium reference panels and a hierarchical prior on effect size heterogeneity between ancestry groups. PGS-FAI improved R-squared for body mass index prediction by 34.7 percent in the West African subgroup and by 21.4 percent in the South Asian subgroup relative to the standard European-trained score. For coronary artery disease-equivalent risk prediction, PGS-FAI achieved area-under-the-ROC-curve values of 0.71 and 0.69 in East Asian and South Asian groups respectively, compared with 0.64 and 0.62 for the standard score. Software implementing the PGS-FAI method has been released as an open-source R package, designated pgsR.fai, available through the Faustino Institute's GitHub repository with a tutorial dataset. The analytical approach is described in a preprint submitted to the Iberian Journal of Human Genetic Epidemiology on January 17th.

Researchers at the Salazar Institute for RNA Biology in Guadalajara have characterized a family of N6-methyladenosine reader proteins, designated the YTHDC-S clade, comprising five paralogs unique to vertebrates that preferentially recognize m6A modifications within a UGGACU context motif found at elevated density in the 3-prime UTRs of genes encoding synaptic proteins. Using individual-nucleotide resolution crosslinking and immunoprecipitation sequencing at 80 million reads per sample across 12 brain regions from post-mortem human donors, the team mapped 84,000 high-confidence YTHDC-S2 binding sites, of which 61,200 overlapped m6A peaks detected by m6A-seq in matched tissue. Depletion of YTHDC-S2 by CRISPRi in induced pluripotent stem cell-derived glutamatergic neurons reduced the stability of 312 synaptic transcripts by a mean of 38.4 percent as measured by metabolic RNA labeling with 4-thiouridine and RNA sequencing. Among the most destabilized transcripts was SHANK-homolog SHNK-3, whose mRNA half-life decreased from 14.2 hours to 5.8 hours upon YTHDC-S2 depletion. Rescue experiments confirmed that the effect was dependent on m6A recognition, as a point mutation in the YTHDC-S2 aromatic cage (W439A equivalent) abrogated RNA binding and transcript stabilization. Dr. Isabela Salazar-Guerrero indicated that the YTHDC-S clade reader proteins represent a new therapeutic target for synaptic dysfunction disorders.

The Nkemdirim Foundation Clinical Genomics Program in Abuja has reported outcomes from a two-year natural history study of 218 individuals with biallelic variants in the MTTL1-homolog MTLH-2 gene responsible for Nkemdirim mitochondrial tRNA synthetase syndrome, a multi-system disorder characterized by sensorineural hearing loss, cardiac conduction defects, and progressive cerebellar atrophy. Participants underwent annual assessments including whole-exome sequencing to confirm genotype, brain MRI volumetry, pure tone audiometry, and 24-hour Holter monitoring. Annual rate of cerebellar cortex volume loss averaged 2.4 percent per year compared with 0.3 percent per year in 80 age-matched controls, with a significant genotype-phenotype correlation observed between predicted pathogenicity scores of MTLH-2 variants and cerebellar atrophy rate. Hearing threshold elevation progressed at a mean of 4.8 decibels per year across all frequencies tested, with higher-frequency thresholds declining more rapidly. Cardiac conduction abnormality, defined as PR interval greater than 200 milliseconds, was present in 74 of 218 participants at baseline and emerged in an additional 38 participants over the two-year follow-up. The natural history data will serve as the historical control arm for a planned interventional trial of a mitochondrially targeted ribonucleoprotein complex designed to restore MTLH-2 tRNA aminoacylation activity in affected tissues.

A research group at the Westholm Institute for Computational Biology in Stockholm has developed a transformer-based deep learning architecture, designated GenomeBERT-W9, pretrained on 2.1 trillion base pairs of genomic sequence from 4,800 vertebrate species using a masked token prediction objective analogous to established language model pretraining. Fine-tuning GenomeBERT-W9 on 680,000 characterized variant effect measurements from saturation genome editing experiments enabled prediction of single-nucleotide variant functional consequences with a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with held-out experimental measurements, outperforming the best prior sequence-based model by 0.14 correlation units. Applied to the clinical variant interpretation task of classifying variants of uncertain significance as pathogenic or benign, GenomeBERT-W9 achieved an area-under-ROC of 0.87 on a benchmark set of 24,000 ClinVar-equivalent variants with confirmed classifications, compared with 0.74 for the leading prior tool. The model identified 12,400 variants in population sequencing databases currently classified as variants of uncertain significance that it predicts with high confidence to be pathogenic, including 847 in genes associated with sudden cardiac death. Dr. Astrid Lindblom-Westholm reported that the model weights and inference code have been released under a permissive license and that an online prediction server accepting VCF-format input files will be operational within 30 days.

Investigators at the Carvalho Center for Somatic Evolution in Porto have profiled somatic mutational landscapes across 14 tissue types in 620 individuals spanning ages 20 to 89 using ultra-deep amplicon sequencing of a 1,200-gene panel at a mean depth of 4,800-fold coverage, identifying a total of 2.1 million somatic single-nucleotide variants with variant allele fractions as low as 0.2 percent. The study found that somatic clone expansion, defined as a clone occupying greater than 1 percent of cells in a tissue sample, increased in frequency from 8.4 percent of participants aged 20 to 29 to 67.3 percent of participants aged 80 to 89. In liver tissue, the most common driver of clonal expansion was a hotspot variant at position 742 of the CTNNB1-homolog CTNNH-3 gene, observed in 48 of 620 donors. In skeletal muscle, mitochondrial DNA deletion accumulation correlated with age with a Pearson coefficient of 0.71, with deletions spanning the arc between nucleotide positions 8,200 and 14,600 most prevalent, consistent with a replication-slippage mechanism facilitated by 8-base-pair direct repeats flanking this region. The team estimated that individuals in the highest quartile of somatic variant burden at age 60 had a 2.8-fold elevated risk of incident hematologic malignancy over the subsequent five years based on linkage to national cancer registry data.

The Ambrosetti Consortium for Protein Folding Diseases at the University of Ferrara has described a CRISPR-Cas13-based RNA knockdown strategy targeting the gain-of-toxic-function mutant transcript of the ATXN3-homolog ATXH-3 gene harboring a polyglutamine expansion of 78 repeats, which selectively reduced mutant transcript abundance by 88.3 percent while preserving wild-type ATXH-3 expression at 94.7 percent of baseline in induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cerebellar neurons from four spinocerebellar ataxia type 3 equivalent patients. Selectivity was achieved by designing crRNA spacer sequences spanning the CAG repeat junction of the mutant allele, exploiting the fact that the expanded repeat generates a secondary structure detectable by Cas13d with greater efficiency than the shorter wild-type repeat. In a mouse model carrying 76 CAG repeats knocked into the Atxh3 locus, intracerebellar administration of an AAV5 vector expressing the Cas13d-crRNA construct at 2 times 10 to the 11th vector genomes per mouse reduced mutant ATXH-3 protein aggregates in Purkinje cells by 71.4 percent at 16 weeks post-injection as measured by immunohistochemical quantification. Rotarod performance improved by 38.7 percent relative to untreated carrier mice, and Purkinje cell density in the cerebellar cortex was preserved at 82 percent of wild-type levels versus 54 percent in untreated carriers. The findings were published in the Ferrara Journal of Molecular Neurotherapeutics on October 19th.

A team at the Bergendal Institute for Environmental Epigenomics in Lund has demonstrated that prenatal exposure to the endocrine disruptor compound TRX-benzofluoranthene at concentrations of 0.3 and 3.0 micrograms per kilogram per day in a rat model induces heritable DNA methylation changes at 312 CpG loci in germ-line tissue that persist through three generations of unexposed offspring, as detected by whole-genome bisulfite sequencing at 25-fold coverage. The most significantly affected locus, designated EpV-BF1, consists of a 1.4-kilobase differentially methylated region in the promoter of the metabolic gene FASNH-4, which showed a 34.7 percentage-point decrease in methylation in F1 sperm and a 28.3 percentage-point decrease in F2 somatic tissues relative to vehicle-exposed controls. Gene expression analysis confirmed that FASNH-4 transcript abundance in F2 liver was elevated 2.8-fold, correlating inversely with methylation at EpV-BF1 with a Pearson r of negative 0.84. The authors propose a model in which TRX-benzofluoranthene-induced recruitment of the demethylase TET-homolog TETH-3 to FASNH-4 during primordial germ cell reprogramming escapes the post-fertilization epigenetic resetting mechanism due to protective boundary elements flanking the EpV-BF1 locus. Dr. Britta Svantesson-Bergendal communicated the findings to the Nordic Society of Epigenetic Toxicology annual symposium in Lund on May 29th.

Scientists at the Mwangi Research Centre for Infectious Disease Genomics in Nairobi have sequenced the complete genomes of 1,840 Plasmodium-homolog Plasmodix falciparens isolates collected from patients presenting with severe malaria in three district hospitals over a 24-month period, identifying signatures of selective sweeps affecting the drug resistance gene DHPS-homolog DHPSH-2 at a frequency of 78.4 percent in the sampled population, up from 41.2 percent in a matched collection from five years prior. A novel nonsynonymous variant at codon 612 of the putative kelch-domain protein KLCH-19, absent from all prior public Plasmodix databases, was identified in 214 isolates at allele frequencies ranging from 0.08 to 1.0 across the three hospitals, and was associated with a 4.1-fold elevation in the minimum inhibitory concentration of the frontline artemisinin-homolog compound WerQ-7 in ex vivo susceptibility assays. Comparative population genomic analysis using 14 summary statistics revealed evidence of a selective sweep spanning 84 kilobases centered on KLCH-19, with nucleotide diversity in this window reduced to 12 percent of the genome-wide average. The KLCH-19 612 variant has been reported to the East African Antimalarial Resistance Surveillance Network and flagged for immediate clinical treatment protocol review. Dr. Wanjiku Mwangi-Kibiru presented these findings at the East Africa Infectious Disease Genomics Summit in Nairobi on August 4th.

The Delphine Biological Institute in Nantes has published a study characterizing the three-dimensional genome organization of human adipose progenitor cells during adipogenic differentiation using high-throughput chromosome conformation capture sequencing at 5-kilobase resolution across six differentiation time points from day 0 to day 14. The analysis identified 4,200 chromatin domain boundaries that shifted position by greater than 25 kilobases during differentiation, of which 1,840 coincided with activation of adipocyte-specific enhancer elements measured by H3K27ac ChIP-sequencing. A particularly striking topological reorganization occurred around the PPARG-homolog PPRGH-3 locus between days 4 and 6, during which a 480-kilobase contact domain encompassing the PPRGH-3 gene and its upstream super-enhancer cluster coalesced from four smaller sub-domains into a single contiguous interaction compartment, coinciding with a 14.3-fold increase in PPRGH-3 transcript abundance. Loop extrusion modeling using the molecular dynamics platform LOOP-MD suggested that this reorganization required increased CTCF-homolog CTCFH-1 occupancy at two flanking insulator elements that gained binding simultaneously with differentiation. Depletion of CTCFH-1 using an auxin-inducible degron system blocked the topological reorganization and reduced PPRGH-3 induction to 3.2-fold. Dr. Camille Bertholet-Delphine reported that the Hi-C datasets have been deposited in the French Epigenomic Data Hub under accession FEH-2024-0512.

A phase II multicenter trial conducted at seven academic medical centers across Japan, coordinated by the Takahashi Institute for Hematopoietic Disorders in Sapporo, has evaluated the safety and efficacy of a lentiviral gene therapy product designated LVX-TH12, which delivers a beta-globin transgene with a T87Q-equivalent amino acid substitution that confers antisickling properties, in 38 transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia patients with a median age of 22 years. At the 24-month primary analysis time point, 31 of 38 patients had achieved transfusion independence, defined as hemoglobin of at least 9 grams per deciliter without transfusion for at least 12 consecutive months. The median total hemoglobin at month 24 was 10.4 grams per deciliter, with the transgene-encoded hemoglobin fraction constituting a median 6.8 grams per deciliter. Integration site analysis by linear amplification-mediated PCR at months 3, 12, and 24 identified a polyclonal integration pattern with no evidence of insertional oncogenesis, with the Shannon diversity index of integration sites averaging 8.1 at month 24. Adverse events included expected post-conditioning cytopenias and two cases of grade 2 hepatotoxicity that resolved with ursodeoxycholic acid supplementation. Dr. Hiroshi Yamamoto-Takahashi, the trial's principal investigator, indicated that a biologics license application will be submitted to the Japanese Pharmaceutical and Medical Devices Agency by the fourth quarter of next year.

Investigators at the Grenoble Center for Structural Genomics have determined the cryo-electron microscopy structure of the Cas12k-homolog CasKH-3 transposase-associated CRISPR effector in complex with its cognate guide RNA, target DNA, and the transposase heterodimer TNPB-homolog TNPBH-7, resolved to an overall resolution of 2.6 angstroms. The structure reveals that CasKH-3 adopts an open-clamp conformation upon target DNA binding, exposing a 14-angstrom channel on the non-target strand that accommodates the TNPBH-7 transposase N-terminal domain, explaining the mechanistic coupling between RNA-directed target recognition and transposon insertion. Comparison with prior Cas12k structures from other bacteria identified a 38-amino-acid insertion loop unique to CasKH-3 that contacts the transposon left end element and is required for coordinated insertion, as demonstrated by deletion mutagenesis reducing integration efficiency by 94.6 percent. The structure-guided design of TNPBH-7 mutations at the CasKH-3 interface increased RNA-directed transposition efficiency from 21.4 percent to 48.7 percent of target sites in E. coli analog RK-12 genomic DNA as measured by Tn-seq. Dr. Frederique Lacombe-Grenier reported that the atomic coordinates and electron density maps have been deposited in the Protein Data Bank under accession code 9GCK-0072 and that the engineered CasKH-3-TNPBH-7 system is being evaluated for application to large transgene insertion without DNA double-strand breaks.

A consortium of researchers from the Pelletier-Gagnon Institute in Quebec City and the Reinhart Foundation in Bern has developed a combinatorial CRISPR screen of 18,400 non-coding regulatory elements to identify enhancers whose deletion confers resistance to the BCR-ABL-homolog kinase inhibitor WerKinib-4 in the chronic myeloid leukemia cell line model KG-1M. The screen, conducted using a paired guide RNA library for precise deletion of each 500-base-pair candidate enhancer, identified 47 enhancers whose deletion increased IC50 for WerKinib-4 by greater than 3-fold. Of these, 12 were found to regulate the expression of the drug efflux gene ABCB-homolog ABCBH-9, whose upregulation is the predominant resistance mechanism. A secondary screen targeting the transcription factor binding sites within these 12 enhancers using base editing identified a 14-base-pair core sequence within enhancer PGR-Enh-3 that is bound by the transcription factor RUNX-homolog RNXH-2 and whose disruption alone is sufficient to confer 4.8-fold resistance. Chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing confirmed that RNXH-2 occupancy at PGR-Enh-3 increased 7.3-fold upon WerKinib-4 treatment in parental KG-1M cells, suggesting that drug-induced transcription factor redistribution contributes to enhancer rewiring during acquired resistance. The findings suggest that PGR-Enh-3 may serve as a biomarker for emerging WerKinib-4 resistance in clinical monitoring programs.

The Manzanero Institute for Gene Circuit Design in Barcelona has reported the construction and in vivo validation of a synthetic gene circuit designated GC-MNZ3 that autonomously senses and corrects elevated uric acid in a mouse model of hyperuricemia by expressing the urate oxidase-homolog enzyme URAOX-7 in a feedback-regulated manner. The circuit comprises a synthetic promoter incorporating four tandem binding sites for the uric acid-responsive transcription factor UXRF-1, a variant of the human urate transporter regulatory domain URSRD engineered in this study, driving expression of a codon-optimized URAOX-7 transgene. In hepatocyte-targeting AAV8-delivered mouse experiments, uric acid concentrations above a threshold of 8.2 milligrams per deciliter induced UXRF-1-dependent GC-MNZ3 activation, reducing uric acid levels by a mean of 4.3 milligrams per deciliter within 48 hours. Below the 8.2 milligrams per deciliter threshold, circuit activity was less than 4 percent of maximum, minimizing inappropriate enzyme expression. Over a 16-week study period in 24 hyperuricemic mice, GC-MNZ3 maintained serum uric acid below 7.0 milligrams per deciliter in 19 of 24 animals compared with 0 of 24 untreated controls. No anti-URAOX-7 antibodies were detected by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay at weeks 4, 8, 12, and 16. Dr. Beatriz Manzanero-Crespi indicated that the circuit design framework has been generalized to other metabolite-sensing applications including hyperbilirubinemia and phenylketonuria.

Scientists at the Lindstrom Institute for Molecular Cardiology in Helsinki have used a pooled CRISPR knockout screen targeting all 1,840 kinases and phosphatases in the human genome to identify regulators of cardiomyocyte hypertrophic signaling in a human iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte model of pressure-overload hypertrophy induced by chronic endothelin-1 homolog treatment. The screen, conducted across three independent biological replicates with each gene targeted by 6 independent sgRNA sequences, achieved greater than 97 percent library coverage at a mean representation of 340 cells per guide. Analysis using the MAGeCK-homolog algorithm identified 84 high-confidence essential regulators of the hypertrophic response, of which 31 were novel and not previously implicated in cardiomyocyte biology. The top hit, an uncharacterized kinase designated CRDKN-14, was validated by individual knockout in 12 independent cardiomyocyte lines, showing 62.3 percent reduction in cell surface area enlargement and 58.7 percent reduction in brain natriuretic peptide-homolog BNPH-3 expression relative to non-targeting controls. CRDKN-14 protein immunoprecipitation coupled to mass spectrometry identified 47 interacting proteins, including MAPK-homolog MPKH-9 and the E3 ubiquitin ligase TRIM-homolog TRMH-21, suggesting a role in post-translational regulation of the calcineurin-NFAT-homolog pathway. Dr. Erika Lindstrom-Karinen presented the screen results at the Helsinki Cardiovascular Genomics Symposium on March 28th.

Researchers at the Obumseli Center for Metabolic Gene Therapy in Enugu have demonstrated successful in vivo base editing of the PAHOMOLOG PAH-7 gene in a murine model of phenylketonuria-analog disorder using an adenine base editor delivered as a ribonucleoprotein complex encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles administered intravenously at a dose of 1.5 milligrams per kilogram. The editing approach targeted a C-to-T transition at position 814 of the PAH-7 coding sequence responsible for an Arg-to-Trp substitution that abolishes enzymatic activity in the murine equivalent of the disorder. A-to-G base editing at the complementary strand position corrected the mutation in 78.4 percent of sequenced hepatocyte alleles as measured by amplicon deep sequencing at 12,000-fold coverage in liver biopsies collected four weeks post-treatment. Plasma phenylalanine-homolog PHA concentrations declined from a pre-treatment mean of 1,240 micromolar to a post-treatment mean of 84 micromolar at the 8-week assessment, within the therapeutic target range of less than 120 micromolar. Liver PAH-7 enzymatic activity, measured in hepatic lysates by a coupled colorimetric assay, recovered to 58.3 percent of wild-type activity in treated mice compared with less than 2 percent in untreated affected mice. The Obumseli team reported that IND-enabling toxicology studies are currently underway in support of a first-in-human trial application.

A team at the Rothermund Institute for Transcriptome Medicine in Leipzig has developed a method for multiplexed in situ single-molecule RNA imaging, designated FISH-RM7, capable of simultaneously detecting 2,400 distinct transcript species in tissue sections with a spatial resolution of 120 nanometers using a combinatorial fluorescent barcode scheme employing five spectrally distinct dyes and four sequential hybridization rounds. In the first application of FISH-RM7 to human pathological tissue, the team profiled 40 colorectal adenoma and 40 matched normal colonic mucosa samples from a retrospective biobank, generating expression measurements for all 2,400 transcripts in greater than 4.2 million individual cells across the 80 samples. Unsupervised clustering of single-cell expression profiles identified 28 epithelial cell states, including a previously undescribed adenoma-specific transit-amplifying progenitor population expressing the marker genes ASCL-homolog ASCL-3 and MYC-homolog MYCH-7 co-expressed in 12.4 percent of adenoma epithelial cells but in fewer than 0.1 percent of normal mucosal cells. This population exhibited significantly elevated expression of the Wnt target AXIN2-homolog AXNH-4 and reduced expression of the differentiation factor CDX2-homolog CDXH-2. Dr. Gerhart Rothermund-Klein indicated that FISH-RM7 reagent kits targeting the 2,400-transcript panel will be made available to academic collaborators under a material transfer agreement.

The Solomonov Institute for Immunogenomics in Novosibirsk has catalogued T-cell receptor beta chain repertoire diversity in peripheral blood from 480 patients with an autoimmune encephalitis-spectrum disorder caused by autoantibodies against the glutamate receptor-homolog GLURH-2, using a high-throughput TCR sequencing protocol achieving a median of 224,000 productive clonotype sequences per sample. The study found that repertoire diversity, measured by Shannon entropy, was significantly reduced in affected patients compared with 240 age-matched healthy controls, with median H values of 12.4 versus 14.8 respectively. A disease-associated clonotype cluster, designated DAC-SOLIN-7, encoding a CDR3 beta sequence of CASSKDTEAFF with amino acid length of 11 residues, was detected at frequencies above 0.1 percent in 147 of 480 patients but in only 2 of 240 controls, a 73-fold enrichment. Structural modeling of DAC-SOLIN-7 in complex with a predicted GLURH-2 peptide-MHC class II complex suggested a binding footprint spanning eight contacts between TCR residues and MHC-bound peptide, consistent with high-affinity recognition. Longitudinal sampling of 64 patients over 12 months revealed that DAC-SOLIN-7 frequency correlated inversely with clinical symptom severity scores, suggesting its potential utility as a disease activity biomarker. The repertoire dataset has been deposited in the Pan-Eurasian Immune Repertoire Database under accession PEIRD-2024-0311.

An investigational mRNA-based therapy for ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency, designated mOTC-RFV6, has demonstrated compelling efficacy data in a first-in-human open-label ascending-dose trial conducted at the Riverfront Center for Metabolic Medicine in Cincinnati, with all 12 participants in the highest dose cohort achieving normalization of plasma ammonia concentrations following a single intravenous infusion of 1.0 milligram per kilogram. The mRNA molecule encodes a codon-optimized, mitochondrial targeting sequence-fused ornithine transcarbamylase-homolog enzyme OTCH-3 and incorporates a proprietary 3-prime UTR element designated StabElement-G9 that extended in vivo mRNA half-life from an estimated 4.6 hours to 18.2 hours as modeled from pharmacodynamic enzyme activity curves in serum. Peak plasma ammonia concentrations, which ranged from 380 to 720 micromolar at baseline, normalized to below 50 micromolar within 96 hours of infusion in the highest dose cohort and remained below the upper limit of the normal range for a median of 22 days. Urinary orotic acid, a secondary biomarker of urea cycle function, declined by a mean of 84.6 percent from baseline at the 7-day assessment. Two participants experienced grade 1 injection-site reactions that resolved within 24 hours. No serious adverse events were observed across all dose cohorts. The trial's data will support design of a repeat-dosing regimen investigation to be initiated within the next six months.

A research group at the Svensson Center for Epigenetic Inheritance in Gothenburg has reported that prolonged caloric restriction in male mice from 12 to 24 months of age induces heritable histone acetylation changes at 1,840 loci in sperm that are detectable in the liver and hypothalamus of F1 offspring at weaning, using a combined ChIP-sequencing and assay for transposase-accessible chromatin sequencing approach. Loci gaining H3K27ac in the sperm of calorically restricted fathers overlapped significantly with regions losing H3K27ac in F1 offspring hypothalamus, suggesting a reciprocal epigenetic relationship. The most robustly affected region, designated HerEpi-CR4, spans 8.4 kilobases in the promoter of the appetite-regulatory gene AGRPH-5 and showed a 2.8-fold decrease in H3K27ac in F1 hypothalamus compared with offspring of ad-libitum-fed fathers, correlating with a 41.7 percent reduction in hypothalamic AGRPH-5 transcript abundance. F1 offspring of caloric-restriction fathers consumed 14.3 percent less food per body weight per day and maintained 12.4 percent lower body weight than F1 offspring of control fathers through 12 months of age under identical ad-libitum feeding conditions. The inheritance was not observed in F2 offspring derived from F1 females backcrossed to control males, suggesting paternal-line transmission limited to one generation. Dr. Lars-Erik Svensson-Lind reported these findings in the Scandinavian Journal of Epigenetic Physiology on July 3rd.

Scientists at the Fuentes Institute for Genetic Diagnostics in Bogota have validated a long-read sequencing-based method, designated DiagSeq-FL4, for the comprehensive resolution of tandem repeat expansions, inversions, and complex structural rearrangements in a panel of 48 repeat-expansion neurological disorders in a single clinical workflow. The method uses adaptive sampling on the NanoPore PacMax-III platform to enrich for reads spanning 48 genomic target regions while simultaneously generating 22-fold background whole-genome coverage, achieving a mean 340-fold enrichment at target loci from 400-nanogram input DNA samples. In a validation cohort of 220 patients with clinically confirmed diagnoses of 14 distinct repeat-expansion disorders, DiagSeq-FL4 achieved 100 percent sensitivity and 99.6 percent specificity for detecting pathogenic expansions, with the single false-negative attributable to a mosaic expansion present in fewer than 8 percent of blood leukocytes. Resolution of repeat sequence interruptions, which affect disease penetrance and severity in several disorders including the ATXH-11 polyglutamine disorder, was achieved at base-pair resolution in 96.4 percent of expanded alleles. The complete DiagSeq-FL4 analysis pipeline, from raw sequencing reads to clinical variant report generation, requires 11.4 hours on a 64-core computational server. The College of Colombian Medical Geneticists has designated DiagSeq-FL4 as the recommended first-tier diagnostic approach for suspected repeat-expansion disorders effective January 1st of next year.

The Auerbach Foundation for Translational Genomics in Vienna has published the first complete characterization of a chimeric antigen receptor-T cell therapy engineered to incorporate a CRISPR-edited genetic safety switch, designated SafeCAR-AUF4, consisting of T cells whose TCR alpha constant region has been replaced with a CAR targeting the leukemia-associated antigen TSLP-homolog receptor TSLPRH-2, while simultaneously inserting an inducible caspase-9-homolog ICAP-3 suicide gene at the AAVS1-equivalent safe harbor locus. In a pilot study of 16 patients with relapsed or refractory TSLPRH-2-positive B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia at the Auerbach Medical Center, SafeCAR-AUF4 cells were infused at doses of 1 times 10 to the 6th, 3 times 10 to the 6th, or 1 times 10 to the 7th cells per kilogram following lymphodepletion with cyclophosphamide-equivalent CYC-3 and fludarabine-equivalent FLUD-7. Of 16 patients, 13 achieved complete remission with measurable residual disease negativity at the 30-day bone marrow assessment. Three patients developed grade 3 or 4 cytokine release syndrome that was fully aborted within a median of 14 hours by administration of the dimerizing agent AP1903-homolog DIMER-Q that activates ICAP-3, demonstrating the clinical utility of the integrated safety switch. SafeCAR-AUF4 cells persisted in peripheral blood at detectable levels in 11 of 13 responding patients at 6 months post-infusion, with a median peak expansion of 142-fold relative to infused dose occurring at day 9.
